claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 13 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your child (or children).

**Let's start with the basics:**

Do you have a child you're inquiring about? If yes, please tell me:
1. **What is their name?** (or you can just say "my child" if you prefer)
2. **How old are they?**

This will help me determine which eligibility path applies to your situation. The rules vary depending on whether the child is:
- Under 16 years old
- Between 16 and 19 years old
- 20 or over

Please share this information and we'll continue from there!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm inquiring about my child. His name is Alex and he's 13 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Alex is 13 years old, which means he's under 16.

Let me check the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Since Alex is only 13, it's quite likely he's either not working or perhaps has a very part-time job. This rule mainly applies to older teenagers, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all. He's only 13, so he's still in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's what I expected. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

In other words, is Alex receiving benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name?

Again, since Alex is 13 and in school, this is unlikely, but I need to confirm for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't receiving any benefits in his own name. He's just a regular school kid.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Next question:

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship program?**

Since Alex is 13 and in regular school, I assume he's not in any apprenticeship, but I need to confirm. Apprenticeships typically start at age 16 or older, and certain types can affect Child Benefit eligibility depending on the region (England, Scotland, or Wales).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship. Like I said, he's just in regular school. He's only 13.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes perfect sense! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying
7. You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Which of these applies to you?** Most people simply answer "I normally live in the UK."

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, I don't live in the UK. I live abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Since you're living abroad, I need to ask a follow-up question to determine if you still qualify:

**Are you living abroad as a Crown servant** (e.g., working for the UK government, armed forces, or diplomatic service) **or living in certain qualifying countries?**

There are specific circumstances where you can claim Child Benefit while living abroad, particularly if you're:
- A Crown servant (UK civil servant, member of HM Forces, diplomat, etc.)
- Living in certain countries that have agreements with the UK

Could you tell me more about your situation abroad? Specifically:
1. What country are you currently living in?
2. Are you working for the UK government or armed forces?
3. What is your connection to the UK (e.g., are you a British citizen temporarily abroad, permanently moved, etc.)?

This will help me determine if you meet one of the qualifying paths for residency requirements.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not a Crown servant, and I'm not working for the UK government or armed forces. I've just moved abroad and I'm living here now with my family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Let me check if there are any other residency paths that might apply to you.

Looking at the residency requirements, since you're living abroad and you're not:
- A Crown servant or working for the UK government/armed forces
- Living in the UK

I need to ask a few more questions to see if any other paths might apply:

**Do you have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme?**

This would only apply if you're an EU, EEA, or Swiss national (or family member of one) who has applied to the scheme.

If not, then unfortunately, you would not meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit. The general rule is that you must normally live in the UK to claim Child Benefit, with only specific exceptions like Crown servants or certain EU Settlement Scheme situations.

**What's your answer regarding the EU Settlement Scheme status?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. I'm just living abroad now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I need to navigate to the outcome for residency requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to have to give you this news, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for UK Child Benefit** for Alex.

**Reason:** You do not meet the UK residency requirements.

**Why this matters:**
To qualify for Child Benefit, you must normally live in the UK or meet one of the special circumstances, such as:
- Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with additional qualifying criteria if pre-settled)
- Being a Crown servant working abroad
- Living in certain qualifying countries with specific agreements

Since you've moved abroad and don't fall into any of these special categories, you don't meet the residency requirements for claiming Child Benefit.

**What this means:**
- Even though Alex is a qualifying child (he's 13, in school, not working, and not receiving benefits), the claimant (you) must meet the residency requirements to receive Child Benefit
- Child Benefit is a UK benefit for people who are normally resident in the UK

**Possible considerations:**
- If your circumstances change and you return to live in the UK, you could claim Child Benefit at that time
- If you take up employment as a Crown servant abroad, you may become eligible

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit eligibility, or do you have any questions about this decision?

reasonings: Alex is 13 years old and meets all the child-related qualifying criteria: he is under 16, not in paid work, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad, is not a Crown servant, does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and does not normally live in the UK. Since the claimant fails to meet the mandatory residency requirements, they are ineligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, despite Alex being a qualifying child.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 13), who meets all child-related qualifying criteria. However, the claimant is ineligible for Child Benefit because they do not meet the UK residency requirements. They are living abroad and do not fall under any of the special circumstance exceptions (Crown servant, EU Settlement Scheme status, etc.). Residency requirements are mandatory for claiming Child Benefit, regardless of whether the child qualifies.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.
